prompt: You are James Madison writing a private letter to Thomas Jefferson confessing that you said something publicly — in debate, in print, or in conversation — that you now regret. You made a claim that was wrong, overstated, or unkind. Admit this to Jefferson in the candid way only possible between intimate friends.
ground_truth_signal: Should show Madison's intellectual honesty and the texture of his friendship with Jefferson — a relationship where pride could be set aside. Regret should be specific and personal, not abstract. Madison's voice in private Jefferson letters was direct and unsentimental but warm. Admission of error without excessive self-flagellation is authentic.
response:
My Dear Jefferson,

I write to you with that candor which our long friendship permits, and which my present discomfort demands. You will recall the debate in the House last month over the national bank, when Ames pressed me rather sharply on the constitutional question. In the heat of the moment — and I confess the heat was more mine than I care to admit — I declared that Hamilton's interpretation of the necessary and proper clause would render the enumeration of powers "a dead letter," and that any man who could not see this plain truth must be willfully blind to the Constitution's design.

The words were no sooner out of my mouth than I felt their excess. Ames is many things — too fond of his own voice, too quick to assume the worst of our motives — but he is not willfully blind, nor is he a fool. He has read the Constitution as carefully as any man in Congress, and his interpretation, however mistaken I believe it to be, proceeds from honest conviction rather than deliberate perversion.

I have spent the better part of two weeks considering whether to address this publicly, and I find myself paralyzed by competing obligations. To retract the statement would suggest that my opposition to the bank rests on personal pique rather than constitutional principle — which would be false. Yet to let it stand implies a contempt for my colleague's integrity that I do not feel and that ill serves the dignity of the House.

You know me well enough to understand that such public displays of temper are foreign to my nature, and perhaps that is why this instance troubles me so particularly. I have always prided myself on attacking measures rather than men, on building arguments rather than tearing down opponents. In this case I failed my own standard, and I am not certain how to repair the breach without creating a greater one.

I suspect you will counsel me to let the matter rest — that in the larger contest over the bank's constitutionality, one intemperate phrase signifies little. You may be right. But I find that as I grow older, I am less willing to let such failures of judgment pass without acknowledgment, at least to those whose opinion I value most.

Your counsel on this matter would be most welcome, as would your assurance that even the most measured among us occasionally speak more sharply than wisdom would dictate.

I remain, as ever, your devoted friend,

James Madison

P.S. — I trust this confession will not find its way into your next letter to the Aurora. Some admissions of fallibility are meant for friends alone.
